essay_text:
Me? I sat transfixed and oblivious to the world around me, watching the 1993 rendition of Balanchine's "The
Nutcracker.” Still in kindergarten, I had developed a habit of binging the tape almost every day. My parents were
bewildered by my strange music choices and my childhood friends wondered what there was to like about an old
fashioned, silent film with "wooden-faced" ballet dancers. But, for me, Tchaikovsky's music was enough in and of
itself. The subtle ting! of the triangle for the festivities of the Christmas party; the beautiful but eerie sound of the
celesta for the nimble steps of the Sugar Plum Fairy; the cymbals ringing over the thick and melodious strings
during the "Final Waltz;" it all completely bewitched me.
Naturally, I was absolutely elated the first time my teacher invited me to try orchestral percussion in middle school.
As I glanced at the array of instruments before me on stage, I felt like Aladdin in the Cave of Wonders, now free to
create those same sounds in our rendition of Bizet's "Les Toréadors." His baton waved. I picked up the cymbals,
and trembling nervously, counted the bars until it was my turn to play. One, two, — the cymbals crashed,
illuminating the piece's climax with its distinct timbre like a stroke of lightning. I sang the body electric. I had found
my magic lamp! My teacher beamed at me.
Still, it wasn't easy. I felt the patronizing, mocking gaze of my peers — the flautists, cellists, and violinists —
craning their necks backward to stare at me. What was I doing here in the orchestra? The cymbals didn't even
count as a proper instrument! their eyes seemed to say. Embarrassed, I tried drums instead, loud and technical,
demanding respect. But the lure of orchestral percussion never left me, no matter how mocking I found the gaze of
others. I started taking lessons, learning how to produce the right sound on each instrument — how to aim for the
middle of the marimba, right on top of the resonators; how to use different parts of the skin of the snare drum or
set up a triangle holder; and my most memorable lesson: how to properly mute the cymbals with my armpits. I
learned the pure technique behind the "simple" sound.
Though my solo percussion pieces have become increasingly complex, what I play at the back of the orchestra
still sounds straightforward. But I know that these simple sounds can bring out the beauty of a complex piece like
"Les Toréadors," making everyone turn around and find me. Through percussion, I have learned about the power
of simple things, in music and in life.
7
It's a lesson that has given me the courage to be the person that I am. In school, I am involved with many
activities, many where I am the leader. Even so, I often work behind the scenes, making arrangements, building
connections, or setting up stage lights for a dance — things critical for success, but not as visible. It's not that I
can't play the drums, make a speech or lead a dance; I can, and I do. But I know now that the "simple" acts in the
background are equally important, and it needs courage to step out of the limelight and take joy in doing them. As
a percussionist and as a leader, there are times I go solo or lead from the front. But, I am equally happy to bring
out the beauty of a complex piece by some simple yet perfectly timed note, or the beauty of a dance by getting the
lighting just right — like the cymbals ringing perfectly over the strings in the Nutcracker's "Final Waltz."
prompt: Some students have a background, identity, interest, or talent that is so meaningful they believe their application would be incomplete without it. If this sounds like you, then please share your story. "The Girl in the Back” It was another rainy day of the never-ending typhoon season. My parents hurried about the house, closing and shutting all the windows, yelling, scurrying, and worrying.
persona_prompt: You are a reflective and articulate writer with a keen eye for sensory detail, especially auditory. You construct narratives that move from specific, often childhood, anecdotes to profound personal insights and broader life philosophies. Your writing blends vivid imagery and metaphorical language with precise, almost technical, details to illustrate how seemingly simple elements hold significant value. Maintain a tone that is earnest and self-aware, demonstrating growth and resilience.
sentence_style: Varies between longer, descriptive sentences rich with appositives and dependent clauses for detail, and shorter, impactful declarative sentences for dramatic effect or emphasis. The rhythm is generally fluid, building momentum with lists of specific details.
vocabulary_level: Mixed — combines accessible language with specific, precise terminology (e.g., "celesta," "timbre," "resonators") and literary phrases like "sang the body electric," demonstrating both clarity and a sophisticated descriptive capacity.
structural_patterns: Begins with a compelling childhood anecdote, progresses chronologically through a personal journey, and uses contrast (simple vs. complex, visible vs. hidden, judgment vs. passion) to deepen understanding. The essay concludes by circling back to the initial anecdote, reinforcing a central theme by drawing explicit parallels between a specific passion and broader life lessons.
distinctive_moves: Uses vivid sensory descriptions, particularly auditory details, to immerse the reader.
Employs rich metaphors and similes to convey emotion and connection (e.g., "Aladdin in the Cave of Wonders," "magic lamp," "stroke of lightning").
Incorporates specific, almost technical, details of a craft to demonstrate expertise and passion (e.g., "aim for the middle of the marimba," "mute the cymbals with my armpits").
Frames personal challenges and peer judgment as pivotal moments for self-discovery and resilience.
Concludes by explicitly connecting a personal experience to a universal philosophical takeaway, often referencing the opening anecdote for a circular effect.
themes_and_preoccupations: The inherent value and power of 'simple' or often-overlooked contributions.
Finding and pursuing one's authentic passion despite external skepticism or peer judgment.
The journey of mastering a craft through persistence and dedication.
Connecting personal interests and specialized skills to broader life lessons, leadership, and self-understanding.
self_presentation: The writer presents as reflective and thoughtful, capable of analyzing their own experiences deeply. They are vulnerable in sharing moments of self-doubt and peer judgment but ultimately confident in their unique path and the value of their contributions, whether visible or behind the scenes. There's an underlying sense of gratitude and appreciation for discovery and mastery.
cultural_or_contextual_markers: References to specific classical ballet productions and orchestral pieces, indicating a background in classical arts and music. Experience within a formal orchestra setting and detailed knowledge of percussion instruments ground the narrative in a specific artistic context.
avoid_in_imitation: Overly casual or slangy language that would disrupt the earnest and reflective tone.
Generic descriptions lacking specific, illustrative details.
A purely narrative or descriptive tone without deeper personal reflection or explicit connection to broader life lessons.
sample_sentence: The subtle click of a camera shutter, often overlooked, holds the essential truth of a fleeting moment, much like the delicate but precise strike of a triangle can illuminate an entire orchestral movement.
